verses.text: Job calls them 'miserable comforters,' whose endless words bring no relief; were they in his place, he says, he would rather strengthen than wound them. He longs for comfort and meets only reproach. The mark of a true friend is to lift, not to crush, the one who is down.

Job calls them 'miserable comforters,' whose endless words bring no relief; were they in his place, he says, he would rather strengthen than wound them. He longs for comfort and meets only reproach. The mark of a true friend is to lift, not to crush, the one who is down.

Job calls them 'miserable comforters,' whose endless words bring no relief; were they in his place, he says, he would rather strengthen than wound them. He longs for comfort and meets only reproach. The mark of a true friend is to lift, not to crush, the one who is down.

Whether he speaks or stays silent, his grief is not eased; God has worn him out, and his very leanness rises as a witness against him. He feels trapped on every side. There are sorrows from which neither speech nor silence gives escape.

Whether he speaks or stays silent, his grief is not eased; God has worn him out, and his very leanness rises as a witness against him. He feels trapped on every side. There are sorrows from which neither speech nor silence gives escape.

Whether he speaks or stays silent, his grief is not eased; God has worn him out, and his very leanness rises as a witness against him. He feels trapped on every side. There are sorrows from which neither speech nor silence gives escape.

He feels torn as by a wild beast and given over into the hands of the ungodly. His anguish pictures God Himself as his attacker. In deep affliction the soul may misread the loving hand as a hostile one.

He feels torn as by a wild beast and given over into the hands of the ungodly. His anguish pictures God Himself as his attacker. In deep affliction the soul may misread the loving hand as a hostile one.

He feels torn as by a wild beast and given over into the hands of the ungodly. His anguish pictures God Himself as his attacker. In deep affliction the soul may misread the loving hand as a hostile one.

He was at ease, but God shook and shattered him, set him up as a target, and ran upon him like a warrior. He feels singled out for assault. Yet what seems like God's enmity is, for the believer, the strange working of His deeper purpose.

He was at ease, but God shook and shattered him, set him up as a target, and ran upon him like a warrior. He feels singled out for assault. Yet what seems like God's enmity is, for the believer, the strange working of His deeper purpose.

Job has sewn sackcloth to his skin and laid his strength in the dust, his face red with weeping — yet his hands are clean and his prayer pure. He suffers, but not for injustice in himself. The godly may be brought very low while their conscience remains undefiled.

Job has sewn sackcloth to his skin and laid his strength in the dust, his face red with weeping — yet his hands are clean and his prayer pure. He suffers, but not for injustice in himself. The godly may be brought very low while their conscience remains undefiled.

Job has sewn sackcloth to his skin and laid his strength in the dust, his face red with weeping — yet his hands are clean and his prayer pure. He suffers, but not for injustice in himself. The godly may be brought very low while their conscience remains undefiled.

'O earth, cover not thou my blood,' he cries, for 'my witness is in heaven.' He appeals beyond men to God Himself. When earth offers no justice, faith looks up to the One who sees all and will vindicate the wronged.

'O earth, cover not thou my blood,' he cries, for 'my witness is in heaven.' He appeals beyond men to God Himself. When earth offers no justice, faith looks up to the One who sees all and will vindicate the wronged.

Mocked by his friends, Job pours out tears to God and longs that someone 'might plead for a man with God.' He yearns for an advocate on high. That longing finds its answer in Christ, who ever lives to intercede for His own.
